frameworks.name: bounded rationality
frameworks.one_line: decision-makers act under finite computational resources, not unlimited reasoning

frameworks.name: information-theoretic / statistical-mechanics formalization of bounded rationality
frameworks.one_line: formal model trading expected utility against an information/resource cost

frameworks.name: subjective expected utility theory
frameworks.one_line: classical baseline assuming unlimited computational resources for choice

core_question: Can an information-theoretic bounded-rationality model account for human choice under time-limited combinatorial decisions?
key_assumptions: decision-makers face resource constraints rather than unlimited reasoning
behavior trades off expected utility against an information/resource cost
agents fall back on learned prior choice patterns when resources are scarce
manipulations: time limit imposed on each decision
combinatorial puzzle as the choice task
key_findings: bounded-rational model fits human choice data across time limits well
fitted parameters separate subjective utility from information capacity
behavior collapses toward learned prior patterns as time pressure increases
interpretations: human deliberation is well described as resource-limited rationality, not full expected-utility maximization
information capacity is a meaningful, recoverable individual-level limit
